title: Majorana fermions at self-generated interfaces
authors: Nikola Prodanov, Sergio Ciuchi, Sergio Caprara
abstract: The Kitaev model describing a one-dimensional topological superconducting chain is known to support two Majorana fermions localized at the systems endpoints when the parameters are tuned to the topological phase. In this work, we investigate the possibility that Majorana fermions may also emerge away from the physical boundaries of the chain. To this purpose, we generalize the Kitaev model by incorporating a local coupling between the electronic density and a classical elastic (lattice) field. This electron-lattice interaction can induce phase separation between superconducting regions characterized by distinct topological invariants, thereby generating internal interfaces that host Majorana bound states. Under these conditions, a dilute gas of Majorana fermions can be realized in the bulk of the system.
summary: The paper explores how an electron-phonon interaction can induce phase separation within a topological superconductor model. This phase separation creates internal interfaces that host Majorana zero modes, allowing for the realization of these exotic quasiparticles in the bulk material. The findings suggest a pathway to engineer topological superconductivity using lattice coupling.
main_problem: The study investigates whether Majorana fermions can emerge within the bulk of a superconducting system, away from physical boundaries, due to self-generated interfaces.
main_result: Majorana fermions are found to exist at internal interfaces formed when the superconducting material phase-separates into regions with distinct topological invariants. These interfaces host zero-energy modes even under periodic boundary conditions.
method: The analysis involves generalizing the Kitaev model by coupling electron density to a classical elastic field, followed by solving self-consistency equations and analyzing the resulting phase diagram in real space.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as a 1D topological superconductor (Kitaev model generalization) coupled to an elastic lattice field. The Hamiltonian includes hopping, pairing, chemical potential terms, and density-dependent coupling to the lattice.
key_observables: Zero-energy modes in the spectrum (Majorana fermions), local spectral density showing zero-bias peaks, and the phase diagram illustrating coexistence regions.
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($\lambda$), electron density ($n$), stiffness ($K$), and superconducting gap parameters ($\Delta$, $t$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed at zero temperature ($T=0$) and often relies on simplifying the problem to homogeneous or dimerized phases in reciprocal space for initial calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams showing topological/non-topological coexistence regions, spectral plots confirming zero modes at interfaces, and density profiles demonstrating polaron trapping at these boundaries.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the generalized model Hamiltonian, deriving self-consistency conditions, analyzing the homogeneous phase transition, and finally using real-space numerical solutions to demonstrate Majorana formation within the coexistence region of the phase diagram.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete mechanism—phase separation driven by electron-lattice coupling—for realizing topological boundary states (Majorana fermions) in a bulk material without needing engineered physical terminations, which is highly relevant for solid-state quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: a) Phase diagram for λ = 2. Red and green lines results from Maxwell construction respectively separating non-topological and topological homoge- neous phases. Black dashed line marks the occurrence of topological criterion when λ = 0.0 and it is the lo- cus of the first order phase transition when λ > 0. Light green (light red) lines are spinodal lines of the red (green) topological (non- topological) homoge- neous phases. Purple dashed line marks the instability line of the homogeneous topological phase towards a CDW (see Sec. III C); this line meets the spinodal line of the homogeneous topological phase at n ≈0.9 and ∆≈0.1, and merges with it. Grey line is the spin- odal line of...

FIG. 2: The chemical potential versus the density of electrons n at ∆= 0.8t and for several equally spaced values of λ, from λ = 1.0 (top curve) to λ = 4.0 (bottom curve). On the green branches (non-topo) the topological criterion of the Kitaev model is not satisfied, while it is met on the red branches (topo). Small dots marks the cohexistence region by Maxwell’s construction. The border between non-topo and topo branches do not depend on λ as predicted by Eq. (6).

FIG. 3: Properties of a numerical solution obtained in the coexistence region of Fig. 1, for λ = 2, ∆= 0.5, with periodic boundary conditions. a) Eigenvalues showing 4 zero modes. b) Charge modulation in the N = 1024 chain. The interface located between two dashed lines is sampled in Fig. 4. c) The modulus squared of the Majorana fermion wave function, as a function of the site index (colors mark the pairwise re- lated zero-modes).

FIG. 4: Local spectral density across the interface shown in Fig. 3 b) (dashed lines), for λ = 2, ∆= 0.5 and a broadening 7 × 10−3. The numerical labels of the various lines identify the site along the chain where the corresponding spectral density is found.

FIG. 5: A charge-density wave generated by the Peierls instability. Here, t = 1, µ = 0.4 (i.e., n ≈0.55), ∆= 0, λ = 2.15, and N = 200. The charge is expressed in units of the electron charge e. The density profile results from computational minimization of the free energy.
